content.assessment: A default-closure-with-mitigation model is the option most aligned with the state's demonstrated preference for centralized cost control, but it directly confronts an organized opposition coalition: the 2022-2023 teacher/parent/student mobilization raised the political cost of reorganization generally, and any explicit closure-by-default framework is likely to be read by that coalition as formalizing exactly the outcome it mobilized against, even with equity mitigations attached. Its legitimacy depends heavily on whether transport and equity guarantees are credible given the current unresolved legal dispute over transport responsibility.
opportunities: Could be paired with existing free under-14 public transport infrastructure introduced in 2023 as a partial implementation base, potentially lowering the marginal political cost of guaranteeing transport
strengths: Explicit institutionalization of consolidation criteria, if paired with resolved transport-responsibility assignment, could increase transparency and reduce the discretionary, ad hoc character of current closure practice
threats: The scale and organization of the 2022-2023 teacher mobilization (50,000+ participants) demonstrates a coalition capable of raising the political cost of any reorganization framed as closure-first, regardless of attached mitigations

A default-closure framing risks being politically read as codifying the disputed and contested outcome that recent local mobilizations (e.g., the 2025 Budapest case) have shown can be reversed under pressure, inviting sustained resistance rather than acceptance
weaknesses: Legal responsibility for pupil transport remains genuinely unresolved between the Interior Ministry and municipalities, undermining the credibility of any transport guarantee central to this proposal's legitimacy
